title: The Clockmaker's Tremor

chart.cc: My hands won't stop shaking. It started about a year ago — just the right one at first. I had to stop winding the city clock. My husband thinks I'm depressed but I told him this feels different.
chart.hpi: The Clockmaker arrived by horse cart, her husband at her side. She has not been able to wind the Grand Meridian Tower clock in fourteen months — the tremor in her right hand makes the delicate mechanism impossible. The shaking is worst when her hands are at rest; it quiets, oddly, when she reaches for something. She walks now with short, shuffling steps and has difficulty rising from chairs. She was treated for depression at the Irongate Infirmary for eight months — the sertraline did nothing.
chart.pmh: Depression (misdiagnosed at the Irongate Infirmary). Hypertension.
chart.fh: No family history of movement disorder.
chart.sh: Retired Master Clockmaker of the Grand Meridian Tower. Lives with her husband in the Cogsworth District. Non-smoker. A glass of wine with supper.

additional_hx: When pressed for more detail, she speaks carefully, as if measuring each word like a gear tooth. The tremor is worst when her hands rest in her lap — it quiets the moment she reaches for something, which she finds cruelly ironic given her trade. Her handwriting has shrunk to near-illegibility over the past year. She rises from chairs by pushing off with both arms, and on cobblestones she moves slowly, afraid of her own feet. She lost her sense of smell two years ago — she mentioned it only when asked, as if it were beneath complaint. The sertraline did nothing. She knew it wouldn't.
pe_findings: General: Masked facies, reduced blink rate, hypophonic voice. Neuro: 4-6 Hz resting tremor right hand > left, classic pill-rolling quality. Cogwheel rigidity bilateral upper extremities. Bradykinesia on finger-tapping. Gait: shuffling, reduced arm swing, difficulty initiating. Postural instability on pull test. No cerebellar signs. MMSE 29/30.
imaging: MRI Brain (non-contrast): No mass lesion, no acute infarct, no hydrocephalus. Mild age-appropriate cortical atrophy. Standard sequences cannot visualize dopaminergic neurons — DaTscan is the appropriate functional study. No midbrain atrophy to suggest PSP.

special_test.name: DaTscan (Dopamine Transporter Scan)
special_test.result: Reduced dopamine transporter uptake in bilateral putamen, asymmetric — right greater than left. Classic comma-to-period sign. Consistent with nigrostriatal dopaminergic degeneration, confirming Parkinson Disease.

tests.name: CBC
tests.result: Normal. Hgb 13.8, WBC 7.2, Plt 224. No anemia or infection.

tests.name: CMP
tests.result: Normal. Creatinine 1.0, glucose 94, LFTs normal. No metabolic cause.

tests.name: EKG
tests.result: Normal sinus rhythm. No arrhythmia. Not directly relevant.

tests.name: Troponin
tests.result: Negative. No cardiac injury.

tests.name: Lipase
tests.result: Normal. No pancreatic involvement.

tests.name: UA
tests.result: Clear, no infection, no protein. Unremarkable.

tests.name: ABG/VBG
tests.result: Normal. pH 7.40, pO2 96, pCO2 40. No respiratory compromise.

tests.name: Coags
tests.result: PT/INR normal. PTT normal. No coagulopathy.

hints: The tremor frequency is 4-6 Hz and disappears with intentional movement. That pattern narrows your differential significantly.
Look beyond the tremor — masked face, reduced blink rate, hypophonic voice, micrographia. These are not psychiatric symptoms.
The sertraline did absolutely nothing. That itself is diagnostic information.
Pill-rolling rest tremor + cogwheel rigidity + bradykinesia + shuffling gait. You have all four cardinal features in front of you.

management.title: Immediate stabilization
management.hint: This patient has been slowly progressive for 14 months. Think about what level of urgency is truly warranted here.

options.feedback_wrong: Abrupt SSRI discontinuation causes withdrawal syndrome — dizziness, nausea, electric shock sensations. Always taper. Never stop abruptly.
This has been progressing for 14 months. Urgent outpatient neurology yes. Emergent inpatient is not warranted.
Thiamine is for Wernicke encephalopathy — confusion, ophthalmoplegia, ataxia. None of those are present here.

options.feedback_correct: Correct. This is a chronic, slowly progressive condition. No emergency exists. A calm and thorough outpatient approach is ideal.

management.title: First-line pharmacotherapy
management.hint: Parkinson Disease involves loss of dopaminergic neurons. Your first-line treatment should address that deficit directly.

options.feedback_wrong: The depression was a misdiagnosis — masked facies and bradyphrenia from Parkinson Disease. More sertraline will not help a dopamine deficiency.

options.feedback_correct: The gold standard. Levodopa crosses the BBB and is converted to dopamine. Carbidopa prevents peripheral conversion, reducing side effects.

options.feedback_wrong: Catastrophically wrong. Haloperidol blocks dopamine receptors — the exact opposite of what this patient needs. It will dramatically worsen every motor symptom. Never.
Propranolol is for essential tremor — an action tremor. This is a rest tremor from dopamine deficiency. Propranolol will not touch it.

management.title: Specialist referral
management.hint: Parkinson Disease is complex and progressive. Who manages it best long-term?

options.feedback_wrong: Brain biopsy for Parkinson Disease is not done in living patients. Lewy bodies are a post-mortem finding. This would be unconscionable.

options.feedback_correct: Also correct — OT is essential. Fine motor tasks, handwriting aids, safety assessment. Both this and movement disorder referral are right answers.

options.feedback_wrong: LP is not indicated in Parkinson Disease. This is a clinical diagnosis supported by DaTscan. CSF analysis will not change management.

options.feedback_correct: Correct. Movement disorder neurologists specialize in optimizing PD management — medication timing, DBS candidacy, motor complications.

management.title: Disposition
management.hint: Is there any reason this stable chronic condition requires inpatient admission?

options.feedback_correct: Exactly right. Stable chronic condition, clear diagnosis, outpatient management with appropriate follow-up.

options.feedback_wrong: DaTscan is an outpatient nuclear medicine study. There is no indication for inpatient admission to obtain it.
No indication for admission. This has been present for 14 months and she is hemodynamically stable. Admission wastes resources.
The diagnosis is established. Withholding levodopa serves no purpose and leaves the patient suffering unnecessarily.

pertinent_positives.hx: pill-rolling 4-6 Hz rest tremor worse at rest and improved with movement, micrographia, shuffling gait, reduced sense of smell, failure to respond to sertraline
pertinent_positives.pe: masked facies, reduced blink rate, cogwheel rigidity, bradykinesia, shuffling gait with reduced arm swing, positive pull test
pertinent_positives.labs: normal CBC, CMP, TSH, B12 ruling out metabolic and nutritional causes
pertinent_positives.special: DaTscan showing reduced dopamine transporter uptake in bilateral putamen consistent with nigrostriatal degeneration

report_template.plan: initiate levodopa/carbidopa 25/100mg TID, taper sertraline, refer to movement disorder specialist, occupational therapy referral, outpatient follow-up in 4-6 weeks

explanation: Parkinson Disease is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder caused by loss of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra pars compacta. Cardinal features: rest tremor (4-6 Hz, pill-rolling), bradykinesia, cogwheel rigidity, postural instability. Levodopa/carbidopa remains the most effective symptomatic treatment. Dopamine-blocking agents are absolutely contraindicated.

explanation_detail.who_gets_it: Adults over 60, slightly more common in men. Associated with prior dopaminergic exposure (antipsychotics can mimic it — drug-induced parkinsonism).
explanation_detail.risk_factors: Age, male sex, pesticide/herbicide exposure, family history (LRRK2, PINK1, Parkin mutations). Protective: smoking, caffeine (oddly).
explanation_detail.typical_presentation: Rest tremor (pill-rolling, 4-6 Hz), bradykinesia, cogwheel rigidity, postural instability. Non-motor: hyposmia, constipation, REM sleep behavior disorder, depression, micrographia.
explanation_detail.moa: Loss of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra pars compacta → reduced dopamine in the striatum → unopposed cholinergic activity → movement disorder. Lewy bodies (alpha-synuclein aggregates) are the pathologic hallmark.
explanation_detail.step1_buzzwords: Rest tremor, pill-rolling, cogwheel rigidity, masked facies, shuffling gait, micrographia, substantia nigra, Lewy bodies, dopamine deficiency.
explanation_detail.lab_buzzwords: DaTscan: reduced dopamine transporter uptake in putamen (comma → period sign). MRI is normal on standard sequences. Clinical diagnosis.
explanation_detail.treatment: Levodopa/Carbidopa (gold standard). MAO-B inhibitors (selegiline, rasagiline) for mild disease. Dopamine agonists (pramipexole, ropinirole). NEVER give dopamine blockers (haloperidol, metoclopramide) — catastrophic worsening.

patient_quote_addl_hx: "The right hand first. Then the left began. I kept telling myself it was fatigue from the mechanisms — the fine work. But I know my hands. These are not tired hands. These are broken ones."

title: The Merchant's Cough

chart.cc: I've had this cough for three months and my wife finally dragged me in. I kept telling her it was the dust from the roads. But last week there was blood in it. And my face looks puffy in the mornings, my collar's too tight.
chart.hpi: The Merchant arrived from the eastern trade routes, visibly thinner than his last visit and reluctant to be here. He has had a persistent dry cough for three months — initially dismissed as road dust from the Ashgate lowlands. Over the past two weeks he has coughed up small amounts of bright red blood on two occasions. His face has taken on a deep red-purple hue that worsens when he leans forward over his ledgers. He has lost twelve pounds without trying and reports his arms have grown weak — he struggled to lift his traveling case this morning.
chart.fh: Father died of lung disease, age 67. Mother: bowel cancer.
chart.sh: Traveling merchant, eastern trade routes. Lives with his wife near the harbor district. Smokes a pipe — 45 pack-year equivalent. Occasional drink.

additional_hx: He answers questions with the minimum number of words possible, arms crossed, as if the consultation is keeping him from something more important. The facial swelling, he admits, has been worsening for six weeks — worse in the mornings, worse when he hunches over his ledgers. His collar is a full inch too tight now. The headaches come with it, mornings mainly. He has lost twelve pounds without meaning to and is irritated by the fact. His arms have gone weak — he had trouble lifting his traveling case this morning and he clearly hates admitting it. The double vision started a month ago. He attributes everything to the roads.
pe_findings: General: Plethoric, cyanotic facies. Marked facial and bilateral upper extremity edema. Dilated, non-pulsatile neck veins. Prominent venous collaterals visible across the chest wall and upper abdomen — flow directed downward. Resp: Decreased breath sounds right upper lobe. No wheeze. Neuro: Proximal muscle weakness bilateral upper > lower extremities. Reflexes diminished globally. Pupils: sluggish reaction bilaterally. No Horner syndrome. No lymphadenopathy detected on palpation — nodes may be mediastinal.
imaging: CXR: Large right hilar mass with widened superior mediastinum. Right upper lobe infiltrate/atelectasis. No pleural effusion. CT Chest with contrast: 6.2cm right hilar mass with direct invasion of the superior vena cava and significant extrinsic compression — SVC lumen reduced by approximately 80%. Extensive mediastinal and paratracheal lymphadenopathy. No pulmonary emboli.

special_test.name: Bronchoscopy with Biopsy
special_test.result: Endobronchial lesion right upper lobe completely obstructing the bronchus. Biopsy: Small cell carcinoma — sheets of small round blue cells, salt-and-pepper chromatin, nuclear molding, scant cytoplasm. Ki-67 >80%. Positive: synaptophysin, chromogranin A, CD56, TTF-1. SCLC extensive stage confirmed.

tests.name: CBC
tests.result: WBC 11.2. Hgb 10.4 (low — anemia of chronic disease). Plt 398 (mildly elevated, reactive).

tests.name: CMP
tests.result: Na 128 mEq/L (LOW — SIADH). K 3.8. Cr 1.1. LFTs mildly elevated. Albumin 2.8 (low).

tests.name: EKG
tests.result: Sinus tachycardia. Low voltage QRS — large mediastinal mass effect. No ischemic changes.

tests.name: Troponin
tests.result: Negative. No myocardial injury.

tests.name: Lipase
tests.result: Normal. No pancreatic involvement.

tests.name: UA
tests.result: Urine Na 62 mEq/L (inappropriately high given low serum Na — SIADH). Urine osmolality 680 mOsm/kg.

tests.name: ABG/VBG
tests.result: pH 7.44, pO2 68 (low) on room air. Mild hypoxemia consistent with mediastinal compression and atelectasis.

tests.name: Coags
tests.result: PT/INR 1.3 mildly elevated — hepatic involvement. PTT normal.

hints: The face is purple-red and swollen, worse in the morning. The neck veins are distended but non-pulsatile. The collar doesn't fit anymore. What structure passes through the mediastinum and would cause all of this if obstructed?
45 pack-years. Hilar mass. Weight loss. Hemoptysis. The chest X-ray widened mediastinum is not a coincidence — it is the diagnosis announcing itself.

Small cell lung cancer has a particular fondness for paraneoplastic syndromes. His hyponatremia, his proximal muscle weakness with facilitation, his facial swelling — these are not separate problems. They are all one disease.
SIADH causing hyponatremia + Lambert-Eaton from voltage-gated calcium channel antibodies + SVC syndrome = classic SCLC paraneoplastic triad. The EMG is your confirmation.

management.title: Immediate management of SVC syndrome
management.hint: His SVC is 80% occluded. His face is turning purple. He has a headache from raised intracranial pressure. What do you do first?

options.feedback_wrong: Diuresis will not relieve SVC obstruction and risks dangerous volume depletion in an already compromised patient. The edema is obstructive, not fluid overload.

options.text: Elevate head of bed, IV dexamethasone, urgent oncology consult
options.feedback_correct: Correct. Head elevation reduces venous pressure to the head. Dexamethasone reduces peritumoral edema acutely. This is a partial oncologic emergency — urgent but not requiring immediate intubation.

options.text: Start anticoagulation for presumed SVC thrombosis
options.feedback_wrong: The CT shows extrinsic compression by tumor, not primary thrombosis. Anticoagulation without histologic diagnosis and in the setting of hemoptysis is dangerous.

options.text: Urgent SVC stenting by interventional radiology
options.feedback_wrong: SVC stenting is sometimes used for severe SVC syndrome but is not the first step — stabilization, diagnosis confirmation, and oncology input come first. Stenting before histology is premature.

management.title: Correct the hyponatremia
management.hint: Na 128. Urine sodium is inappropriately elevated. What is the mechanism and how do you correct it safely?

options.feedback_wrong: Hypertonic saline is reserved for symptomatic severe hyponatremia (seizures, coma, Na <120). Na of 128 without neurological symptoms does not warrant hypertonic saline — too rapid correction risks osmotic demyelination syndrome.

options.feedback_correct: Correct. SIADH treatment is free water restriction, not sodium supplementation. Giving salt will not fix the underlying ADH excess — the kidneys will just excrete it.

options.feedback_wrong: In SIADH, giving normal saline can paradoxically worsen hyponatremia — the kidneys excrete the sodium but retain the free water, worsening the dilution.
Fludrocortisone is for adrenal insufficiency with hyponatremia. This is SIADH from ectopic ADH production by the tumor. Completely different mechanism.

management.title: First-line treatment for extensive stage SCLC
management.hint: Small cell lung cancer, extensive stage. It has already seeded — SVC involvement, likely liver mets. What is the standard of care?

options.feedback_wrong: EGFR-targeted therapy is for non-small cell lung cancer with EGFR mutations. SCLC does not harbor EGFR mutations and does not respond to EGFR inhibitors.
SBRT is for early-stage non-small cell lung cancer. Extensive stage SCLC requires systemic therapy. Local radiation does not address micrometastatic disease.

options.text: Surgical resection of right hilar mass
options.feedback_wrong: Surgery has no role in extensive stage SCLC. SCLC is a systemic disease at presentation in most cases — the problem is not just the primary tumor. Chemotherapy is the backbone of treatment.

options.feedback_correct: Correct. EP (etoposide + platinum) with atezolizumab (PD-L1 inhibitor) is the current first-line for extensive stage SCLC based on the IMpower133 trial. SCLC is exquisitely chemo-sensitive initially.

management.title: Disposition
management.hint: SVC syndrome, Na 128, hemoptysis, O2 94%. Is this patient safe to go home?

options.feedback_wrong: Absolutely not. This patient has SVC syndrome, oxygen saturation of 94%, hemoptysis, and a sodium of 128. Sending him home risks acute decompensation from worsening SVC obstruction or hyponatremic seizure.

options.text: Admit to ICU for immediate chemo initiation
options.feedback_wrong: Chemotherapy is not started without histologic confirmation. You need a tissue diagnosis first. Admitting to the general oncology floor for workup and stabilization is the appropriate level of care.

options.text: Admit — oncology, IV dexamethasone, fluid restriction, urgent CT guided biopsy planning
options.feedback_correct: Correct. This is a new diagnosis of extensive stage SCLC with active SVC syndrome, hemoptysis, and hyponatremia. Inpatient admission is mandatory for stabilization and expedited workup.

options.text: Transfer to thoracic surgery for emergent resection
options.feedback_wrong: Surgery has no role in SCLC management and would be catastrophic without histologic diagnosis. Extensive stage SCLC is managed medically.

report_template.pmh: hypertension, GERD, and 45 pack-year smoking history
report_template.presenting: a 3-month progressive cough with hemoptysis, 12 lb unintentional weight loss, and facial swelling

pertinent_positives.hx: progressive dry cough, hemoptysis, 12 lb weight loss, morning facial swelling worse with bending forward, tight collar, morning headaches, proximal muscle weakness, diplopia, 45 pack-year smoking history
pertinent_positives.pe: plethoric cyanotic facies, bilateral upper extremity and facial edema, dilated non-pulsatile neck veins, chest wall venous collaterals, decreased right upper lobe breath sounds, proximal muscle weakness with diminished reflexes
pertinent_positives.labs: hyponatremia Na 128 with inappropriately elevated urine sodium consistent with SIADH, anemia Hgb 10.4, mildly elevated LFTs
pertinent_positives.imaging: CXR and CT chest showing 6.2cm right hilar mass with 80% SVC compression and mediastinal lymphadenopathy
pertinent_positives.special: bronchoscopy biopsy confirming small cell carcinoma with synaptophysin and chromogranin A positivity, Ki-67 >80%

report_template.assessment: Small Cell Lung Cancer (SCLC), Extensive Stage with SVC syndrome, SIADH, and Lambert-Eaton Myasthenic Syndrome
report_template.plan: admit for stabilization, IV dexamethasone, fluid restriction for SIADH, urgent oncology consult, expedite tissue diagnosis, initiate carboplatin/etoposide/atezolizumab upon histologic confirmation

explanation: Small Cell Lung Cancer (SCLC) is a high-grade neuroendocrine carcinoma strongly associated with heavy smoking. It is characterized by rapid doubling time, early metastasis, and remarkable chemosensitivity. Extensive stage SCLC (beyond one hemithorax) is treated with carboplatin/etoposide + atezolizumab. Paraneoplastic syndromes are common: SIADH (ectopic ADH), Lambert-Eaton (VGCC antibodies), Cushing syndrome (ectopic ACTH). SVC syndrome occurs when mediastinal tumor compresses the superior vena cava — facial plethora, arm edema, dilated non-pulsatile neck veins, and morning headache are hallmarks.

explanation_detail.who_gets_it: Heavy smokers (>30 pack-years), typically men over 60. SCLC accounts for ~15% of all lung cancers but is the most aggressive subtype.
explanation_detail.risk_factors: Smoking (almost universal — >95% of cases). Radon exposure. Occupational carcinogens (asbestos, arsenic). Prior radiation.
explanation_detail.typical_presentation: Central hilar mass, rapid doubling time, early metastasis. Paraneoplastic syndromes in up to 20%: SIADH (hyponatremia), Lambert-Eaton (proximal weakness, facilitation), Cushing (ectopic ACTH). SVC syndrome when mediastinal nodes compress SVC.
explanation_detail.moa: Neuroendocrine tumor arising from Kulchitsky cells of the bronchial epithelium. Expresses synaptophysin, chromogranin A, CD56. Rapid proliferation (Ki-67 >50%). Ectopic hormone production causes paraneoplastic syndromes.
explanation_detail.step1_buzzwords: Small blue cells, salt-and-pepper chromatin, nuclear molding, neuroendocrine markers, SIADH, Lambert-Eaton, SVC syndrome, hilar mass, never surgical.
explanation_detail.lab_buzzwords: Hyponatremia + high urine sodium (SIADH). Lambert-Eaton: facilitation on repetitive nerve stimulation (opposite of MG). ACTH-independent Cushing: very high ACTH, no dexamethasone suppression.
explanation_detail.treatment: Extensive stage: Carboplatin + Etoposide + Atezolizumab (IMpower133). Limited stage: concurrent chemoradiation + prophylactic cranial irradiation. Surgery: no role in extensive stage. Highly chemo-sensitive initially but relapses almost universally.

patient_quote_addl_hx: "I've crossed the Ashgate lowlands in a broken cart with a fever and kept moving. I don't come to doctors. My wife packed my bag. I didn't even know she'd done it until she handed it to me at the door."

title: The Engineer's Fever

chart.cc: I've had a fever every single night for three weeks. I thought it was just a bad flu that wouldn't quit. But I'm exhausted, I've lost weight, and two days ago this painful red spot appeared on my finger. My wife said my eyes looked yellow. I'm scared something is seriously wrong.
chart.hpi: The Engineer arrived pale and drenched in night sweat, having lost nine pounds in three weeks. He had a routine tooth cleaning at the guild dentist five weeks ago — his gums bled considerably during the procedure. The fevers began a fortnight later, nightly, with drenching sweats by morning. He noticed a painful red nodule on his right index fingertip two days ago and dark lines beneath two fingernails. His wife remarked yesterday that his eyes looked faintly yellow. He has developed a sharp chest pain that worsens when he breathes deeply.
chart.pmh: Bicuspid aortic valve — found incidentally at age 22. Never required intervention.
chart.sh: Civil engineer, works the construction sites of the new Ferrovane bridge. Lives with his wife. Non-smoker. A few drinks a week. Denies injection drug use.

additional_hx: He is cooperative and forthcoming, asking clarifying questions as if this were a technical problem to be solved collaboratively. He remembers the dental visit precisely — fifth week ago, Tuesday afternoon, the guild dentist on Meridian Lane, gums bled more than usual. He started feeling feverish about two weeks later and assumed it would pass. He has been tracking his temperature on a small chart he brought with him — nightly spikes to 38.8, breaking by morning. The painful spot on his finger appeared two days ago and he finds it genuinely puzzling. He has not yet connected any of this into a single picture, and when you mention the heart, he looks up with sudden attention.
pe_findings: General: Ill-appearing, sweating, pale. Cardiovascular: Hyperdynamic precordium. New harsh diastolic murmur grade III/VI at left sternal border — not present on prior echocardiogram. JVP mildly elevated. Skin: Osler nodes — tender erythematous nodules on right index and middle fingertip pads. Splinter hemorrhages under right index and middle fingernails (3 total). Janeway lesions — flat, non-tender, hemorrhagic macules on bilateral thenar eminences. Eyes: conjunctival petechiae bilaterally. Fundoscopy: Roth spots — white-centered hemorrhages bilateral retinae. Mild scleral icterus. Spleen palpable 2 cm below costal margin. No peripheral edema. No clubbing yet.
imaging: CXR: Multiple bilateral peripheral nodular opacities — septic emboli pattern. Mild cardiomegaly. Transthoracic Echocardiogram (TTE): Bicuspid aortic valve with a 1.4cm oscillating vegetation on the non-coronary cusp. Severe aortic regurgitation — wide pulse pressure confirmed. EF 55%. No perivalvular abscess on TTE (TEE recommended to exclude).

special_test.name: Blood Cultures x3
special_test.result: All 3 sets positive within 12 hours: Streptococcus viridans (mitis group) — gram-positive cocci in chains. Penicillin MIC 0.06 (fully susceptible). Ampicillin susceptible. Classic cause of native valve endocarditis post-dental procedure.

tests.name: CBC
tests.result: WBC 16,800 with 88% PMNs and left shift. Hgb 9.8 (low — anemia of chronic inflammation). Plt 142 (low-normal).

tests.name: CMP
tests.result: Cr 1.6 (elevated — immune complex GN). BUN 28. Tbili 2.4 (mildly elevated). LFTs mildly elevated. Albumin 2.6.

tests.name: EKG
tests.result: Sinus tachycardia rate 104. New PR interval prolongation to 220ms — concerning for perivalvular abscess extension. Urgent TEE recommended.

tests.name: Troponin
tests.result: 0.04 (mildly elevated) — demand ischemia in setting of severe aortic regurgitation and tachycardia.

tests.name: Lipase
tests.result: Normal. No pancreatic involvement.

tests.name: UA
tests.result: RBC 45/hpf (HIGH — microscopic hematuria). RBC casts present — immune complex glomerulonephritis. Protein 2+. Classic endocarditis finding.

tests.name: ABG/VBG
tests.result: pH 7.42, pO2 82 on room air. Mild hypoxemia from septic pulmonary emboli.

tests.name: Coags
tests.result: PT/INR 1.4 (mildly elevated). PTT 38 (mildly elevated). Hepatic involvement and malnutrition.

hints: Fever for 3 weeks, a dental procedure 5 weeks ago without prophylaxis, a known bicuspid aortic valve, and a new diastolic murmur. The diagnosis is written plainly if you read the timeline.
Osler nodes, Janeway lesions, Roth spots, splinter hemorrhages, microscopic hematuria with RBC casts. These are not separate findings — they are one unifying syndrome with a name.
The wide pulse pressure (122/48) is telling you the aortic valve is incompetent. The vegetation on echo confirms it. Blood cultures will tell you the organism and guide 4-6 weeks of antibiotics.

Duke Criteria: fever (major), vegetation on echo (major), Osler nodes (minor), Janeway lesions (minor), splinter hemorrhages (minor), predisposing valve lesion (minor). You are well past the threshold for definite endocarditis.

management.title: Immediate management
management.hint: He has been on azithromycin for a week with no improvement. What is your first priority before starting the right antibiotics?

options.feedback_wrong: Infective endocarditis requires IV antibiotics for 4-6 weeks. Oral therapy is not appropriate for native valve endocarditis with vegetation. This patient requires admission.

options.text: Urgent cardiac surgery consultation for valve replacement
options.feedback_wrong: Surgery is indicated for severe complications (heart failure, abscess, emboli, uncontrolled infection) — not as the first step. Medical therapy is initiated first. Surgery timing is decided after stabilization and culture results.

options.text: Draw 3 sets of blood cultures then start IV antibiotics
options.feedback_correct: Correct and critical. Blood cultures must be drawn before antibiotics whenever possible. In stable endocarditis you have time to get cultures first — this guides your entire 4-6 week treatment plan. Rushing antibiotics before cultures destroys your ability to tailor therapy.

options.feedback_wrong: Do not start antibiotics before blood cultures in stable endocarditis. He is not in septic shock. He can wait 15 minutes for cultures. Starting vancomycin blindly eliminates your ability to identify the organism and optimize therapy.

management.title: Antibiotic regimen for Strep viridans endocarditis
management.hint: Strep viridans, fully penicillin susceptible, native valve. What is the guideline-recommended regimen?

options.feedback_wrong: Vancomycin is appropriate for penicillin-allergic patients or MRSA. This organism is fully penicillin susceptible — using vancomycin is inferior to beta-lactam therapy and unnecessarily broad.
Daptomycin is inactivated by pulmonary surfactant and cannot be used when pulmonary involvement is present (septic emboli). It is not first-line for Strep viridans endocarditis.
He was already on azithromycin and worsened. Macrolides have poor activity against Strep viridans in a biofilm-protected cardiac vegetation. IV therapy is mandatory.

options.text: IV Penicillin G or Ceftriaxone x4 weeks
options.sub: Drug of choice for susceptible Strep viridans
options.feedback_correct: Correct. Penicillin-susceptible Strep viridans (MIC ≤0.12) on a native valve is treated with penicillin G or ceftriaxone for 4 weeks. Gentamicin can be added for the first 2 weeks to shorten treatment in uncomplicated cases.

management.title: PR prolongation on EKG — next step
management.hint: New PR prolongation to 220ms in a patient with aortic valve endocarditis. What does this mean and what do you do?

options.feedback_correct: Correct and urgent. New PR prolongation in aortic valve endocarditis indicates possible extension into the aortic root with perivalvular abscess — the abscess can interrupt the conduction system. TEE is far more sensitive than TTE for detecting perivalvular complications. Abscess is a surgical indication.

options.text: Reassure — first-degree AV block is benign
options.feedback_wrong: A new PR prolongation in the context of aortic valve endocarditis is never benign — it is a red flag for perivalvular extension until proven otherwise. This is an indication for urgent TEE and possible surgery.
Extending antibiotic duration empirically does not address the question of whether a perivalvular abscess has formed. You need imaging to answer that question, not more antibiotics.
Do not place a pacemaker in active endocarditis — the pacemaker wire becomes a nidus for further infection. The underlying cause (potential abscess) must be diagnosed and treated first.

management.title: Disposition
management.hint: Vegetation confirmed, new murmur, EKG changes, septic emboli on CXR. Where does this patient go?

options.text: Discharge with PICC line for outpatient IV antibiotics
options.feedback_wrong: OPAT is appropriate only for stable, uncomplicated endocarditis after initial inpatient stabilization and when the organism and susceptibilities are known. This patient has active complications — PR prolongation, septic emboli — and is not a candidate for early discharge.

Surgery is not emergent here. He is hemodynamically stable. Indications for urgent surgery include heart failure, uncontrolled infection, abscess, or recurrent emboli despite antibiotics. He needs TEE first to clarify anatomy before any surgical decision.

options.text: Admit to cardiology with cardiac surgery on standby
options.feedback_correct: Correct. Native valve endocarditis with a visible vegetation, severe AR, PR prolongation, and septic emboli requires inpatient admission to cardiology with cardiac surgery availability. 4-6 weeks of IV antibiotics, daily EKGs, serial echos, and monitoring for surgical indications.

options.text: Admit to general medicine floor only
options.feedback_wrong: The complexity of this case — new severe AR, EKG changes suggesting possible abscess, septic emboli — requires cardiology input and cardiac surgery availability. General medicine alone is insufficient.

report_template.pmh: bicuspid aortic valve diagnosed at age 22
report_template.presenting: 3 weeks of daily fevers, night sweats, and 9 lb weight loss following a dental cleaning without antibiotic prophylaxis

pertinent_positives.hx: fever 3 weeks duration, dental procedure 5 weeks ago without prophylaxis, known bicuspid aortic valve, pleuritic chest pain, painful fingertip nodules, dark lines under fingernails
pertinent_positives.pe: new harsh diastolic murmur, Osler nodes on fingertip pads, splinter hemorrhages, Janeway lesions on thenar eminences, Roth spots on fundoscopy, splenomegaly, wide pulse pressure 122/48
pertinent_positives.labs: leukocytosis with left shift, anemia, elevated creatinine, microscopic hematuria with RBC casts on UA consistent with immune complex glomerulonephritis
pertinent_positives.imaging: TTE showing 1.4cm vegetation on bicuspid aortic valve with severe aortic regurgitation, septic emboli on CXR
pertinent_positives.special: all 3 blood culture sets positive for Streptococcus viridans, penicillin susceptible

report_template.assessment: Infective Endocarditis (native valve, Strep viridans) with SVC syndrome, immune complex glomerulonephritis, and septic pulmonary emboli
report_template.plan: blood cultures x3 before antibiotics, IV penicillin G or ceftriaxone x4 weeks, urgent TEE to evaluate for perivalvular abscess given PR prolongation, cardiac surgery consult, admit to cardiology

explanation: Infective endocarditis (IE) is infection of the cardiac endothelium, most commonly involving heart valves. Risk factors include structural valve disease (bicuspid aortic valve), dental procedures without prophylaxis, and IV drug use. Strep viridans is the classic causative organism post-dental procedure. The Duke Criteria (major: positive blood cultures, echo vegetation; minor: fever, vascular phenomena, immunologic phenomena, predisposing condition) guide diagnosis. Peripheral stigmata: Osler nodes (painful, immune complex), Janeway lesions (painless, septic emboli), splinter hemorrhages, Roth spots. Treatment: 4-6 weeks IV antibiotics guided by cultures. Surgical indications: heart failure, abscess, uncontrolled infection, recurrent emboli.

explanation_detail.who_gets_it: Structural valve disease (bicuspid AV, MVP, rheumatic), IV drug users (tricuspid), prosthetic valves, recent dental/invasive procedures without prophylaxis.
explanation_detail.risk_factors: Structural heart disease, IV drug use, poor dentition, central venous catheters, immunosuppression, hemodialysis.
explanation_detail.typical_presentation: Fever + new murmur = endocarditis until proven otherwise. Peripheral stigmata: Osler nodes (tender, fingers — immune complex), Janeway lesions (painless, palms/soles — septic emboli), splinter hemorrhages, Roth spots (retinal). Embolic complications: stroke, renal infarct, septic pulmonary emboli.
explanation_detail.moa: Bacteremia seeds damaged endothelium → vegetation forms (platelets, fibrin, bacteria) → local destruction + systemic emboli + immune complex deposition. Strep viridans post-dental (native valve). S. aureus most aggressive (IV drug use, prosthetic). Enterococcus GI/GU source.
explanation_detail.step1_buzzwords: Duke criteria (2 major OR 1 major + 3 minor), Osler nodes, Janeway lesions, Roth spots, splinter hemorrhages, new murmur, positive blood cultures, vegetation on echo.
explanation_detail.lab_buzzwords: Blood cultures x3 before antibiotics (most important step). UA: RBC casts (immune complex GN). New PR prolongation → perivalvular abscess → urgent TEE. Echo vegetation = major Duke criterion.
explanation_detail.treatment: Native valve Strep viridans: IV Penicillin G or Ceftriaxone x4 weeks. S. aureus: IV Nafcillin (MSSA) or Vancomycin (MRSA) x6 weeks. Surgery indications: heart failure, abscess, uncontrolled infection, recurrent emboli despite antibiotics.

patient_quote_addl_hx: "I actually looked up my symptoms last week. I thought maybe it was just a virus going around the construction site. My wife said I should come in sooner. I kept saying it wasn't a big deal. Is it a big deal?"

title: The General's Episodes

chart.cc: I don't need to be here. My aide insisted. I've had a few dizzy spells — nothing a soldier can't manage. Though I'll admit the last one knocked me off my feet.
chart.hpi: The General was escorted in by his aide, who looked considerably more alarmed than the patient. He has had episodic attacks over the past eight months — each lasting twenty to forty minutes — consisting of a severe pounding headache, profuse sweating, and a sensation that his heart is 'trying to escape his chest.' He initially attributed these to battlefield stress and the pressures of his current campaign. The episodes have increased in frequency: once monthly at first, now nearly weekly. The last attack occurred this morning when he bent down to retrieve a map from the floor — he collapsed briefly and struck his desk. His face remains flushed even now, at rest.
chart.pmh: Hypertension — diagnosed two years ago, poorly controlled on medication. No prior cardiac history.
chart.fh: Mother died of 'a tumor near the kidney,' details unknown. No known cardiac family history.
chart.sh: Military commander, Ferrovane Northern Division. Lives in the officer's quarters at the garrison. Smokes a pipe occasionally. Drinks at regimental dinners.

additional_hx: He describes the episodes only when directly asked, in clipped military language, as if filing a damage report on someone else's equipment. The attacks come without warning — a sudden hammering behind the eyes, then the sweating, then the heart going wild. They last perhaps half an hour and then vanish completely, which is part of why he dismissed them. He has had seven or eight in the past two months. This morning's was triggered by bending to retrieve a map from the floor — he straightened up, the room tilted, and he struck his desk on the way down. He lost perhaps thirty seconds. He does not call this a collapse. He calls it a 'brief interruption.' His mother died of something near the kidney — he doesn't know what, he was overseas. He has lost eight pounds and attributes it to the recent campaign's rations.
pe_findings: General: Flushed, diaphoretic, visibly anxious despite his attempts to appear composed. BP 188/112 right arm, 186/110 left arm — symmetric. HR 108, irregular with frequent palpitations. Skin: warm, clammy, facial flushing. Abdomen: vague fullness in right upper quadrant on deep palpation — patient winces. No palpable mass but tenderness noted. No carotid bruits. Fundoscopy: mild hypertensive retinopathy. No focal neurological deficits. No lymphadenopathy.
imaging: CT Abdomen/Pelvis with contrast: 4.8cm heterogeneous right adrenal mass with central necrosis and peripheral enhancement — highly suspicious for adrenal neoplasm. No liver metastases identified. No lymphadenopathy. Left adrenal gland normal. Incidental note: mild atherosclerosis of the abdominal aorta.

special_test.name: 24-Hour Urine Metanephrines
special_test.result: Urine metanephrines: 4,820 mcg/24hr (normal <900). Urine normetanephrines: 3,110 mcg/24hr (normal <600). Urine catecholamines markedly elevated. Plasma free metanephrines: 2.8 nmol/L (normal <0.5). Results confirm biochemical diagnosis of pheochromocytoma. Genetic testing recommended (RET, VHL, SDHB mutations).

tests.name: CBC
tests.result: WBC 11.4 (mildly elevated — stress response). Hgb 15.2 (normal). Plt 312 (normal). Hematocrit mildly elevated — possible volume contraction from catecholamine-induced vasoconstriction.

tests.name: CMP
tests.result: Glucose 148 (HIGH — catecholamines inhibit insulin release). Cr 1.2 (normal). BUN 18. LFTs normal. Na 141, K 3.4 (mildly low — catecholamine effect). No metabolic acidosis.

tests.name: EKG
tests.result: Sinus tachycardia rate 108. LVH voltage criteria. Diffuse ST changes consistent with catecholamine-induced cardiomyopathy. Frequent PVCs. No STEMI pattern.

tests.name: Troponin
tests.result: Troponin I 0.08 (mildly elevated) — demand ischemia and catecholamine-induced myocardial injury. Not a primary ACS pattern. Serial troponins recommended.

tests.name: Lipase
tests.result: Normal. No pancreatic involvement.

tests.name: UA
tests.result: Glucose 2+ (spillover from hyperglycemia). No RBCs, no casts, no protein. Urine appears concentrated. No infection.

tests.name: ABG/VBG
tests.result: pH 7.46 (mildly alkalotic). pO2 94 on room air. pCO2 36. Mild respiratory alkalosis — anxiety/hyperventilation during paroxysm. No hypoxemia at rest.

tests.name: Coags
tests.result: Normal. PT/INR 1.0. PTT 28. No coagulopathy.

hints: The episodes last twenty to forty minutes and then completely resolve. Between attacks he is entirely normal. What diagnosis presents in paroxysms rather than continuously?
Triggered by bending forward, exertion, emotional stress. Headache, sweating, palpitations — the classic triad. His blood pressure during an attack would be extraordinary.
The family history mentions his mother died of 'a tumor near the kidney.' The CT shows a right adrenal mass. The glucose is elevated without diabetes. Catecholamines do this.
Pheo rule: alpha-block first, then beta-block. Never give beta-blockers alone — you remove the vasodilatory beta-2 effect and leave unopposed alpha vasoconstriction. Hypertensive crisis ensues.

management.title: Immediate blood pressure management
management.hint: BP 188/112 with a suspected catecholamine-secreting tumor. What do you reach for first — and what do you absolutely avoid?

options.feedback_correct: Correct. Alpha-blockade must come first in pheochromocytoma. Phenoxybenzamine (oral, non-selective) or phentolamine (IV for acute crisis) controls the catecholamine-driven hypertension safely. Beta-blockers are added only after alpha-blockade is established.

options.feedback_wrong: Never give beta-blockers first in pheochromocytoma. Blocking beta-2 vasodilation leaves unopposed alpha-1 vasoconstriction — you will precipitate a catastrophic hypertensive crisis. This is one of the classic dangerous drug errors in medicine.

Labetalol is combined alpha/beta blockade but its beta effect dominates. In pheochromocytoma it is not the preferred agent — the ratio of beta to alpha blockade is not adequate. Pure alpha-blockade first is the standard.

Amlodipine has already failed to control his blood pressure for two years. A calcium channel blocker alone is insufficient for catecholamine-driven hypertension of this magnitude. This is not the right move.

management.title: Confirm the biochemical diagnosis
management.hint: You suspect pheochromocytoma. The CT shows an adrenal mass. What do you need before surgery can be planned?

options.text: Plasma free metanephrines or 24-hr urine metanephrines
options.feedback_correct: Correct. Plasma free metanephrines have >97% sensitivity for pheochromocytoma. 24-hour urine metanephrines are slightly less sensitive but highly specific. Either is appropriate. Biochemical confirmation is mandatory before surgical planning.

options.feedback_wrong: Adrenal biopsy of a suspected pheochromocytoma is absolutely contraindicated. Needle manipulation of the tumor can trigger a massive catecholamine release and fatal hypertensive crisis. Never biopsy a suspected pheo.
The clinical picture — paroxysmal hypertension, sweating, palpitations, adrenal mass — is pheochromocytoma, not Cushing syndrome. Cortisol testing is not the priority here.

Operating on an unprepared pheochromocytoma patient is dangerous. Without adequate alpha-blockade preoperatively, surgical manipulation of the tumor causes a massive catecholamine surge on the table. Biochemical confirmation and medical preparation come first.

management.title: Preoperative preparation
management.hint: Surgery is planned. How long must you alpha-block before it is safe to operate?

options.text: Alpha-blockade x 10-14 days, then add beta-blocker, high-salt diet
options.feedback_correct: Correct. Phenoxybenzamine for 10-14 days allows adequate alpha-blockade and volume expansion (catecholamines cause chronic vasoconstriction and volume depletion). High-salt diet and IV fluids help restore intravascular volume. Beta-blockers added only after alpha-blockade is established — to control reflex tachycardia.

options.text: Operate within 48 hours — the mass is large
options.feedback_wrong: Size does not change the requirement for preoperative preparation. Operating without adequate alpha-blockade risks intraoperative hypertensive crisis, arrhythmia, and death. 10-14 days of preparation is not negotiable.

options.text: Start beta-blocker first to control tachycardia
options.feedback_wrong: Beta-blockers before alpha-blockade is the cardinal error in pheochromocytoma management. You will cause unopposed alpha vasoconstriction and potentially fatal hypertension. Alpha first. Always.

options.text: Radiation therapy to shrink the mass preoperatively
options.feedback_wrong: Pheochromocytoma is not treated with radiation preoperatively. The standard approach is medical preparation followed by laparoscopic adrenalectomy. Radiation has no role here.

management.title: Disposition
management.hint: BP 188/112, episodic crises, confirmed adrenal mass. Where does the General go?

options.feedback_correct: Correct. This patient requires inpatient admission for blood pressure stabilization, biochemical confirmation, and preoperative preparation. Endocrinology and surgical oncology consults are essential. He should not leave the building.

options.feedback_wrong: A BP of 188/112 with an uncontrolled catecholamine-secreting tumor and a history of collapse this morning is not an outpatient situation. The next paroxysm could be fatal. He stays.

options.text: Discharge with increased amlodipine and aspirin
options.feedback_wrong: Amlodipine has failed for two years. Aspirin does nothing for a pheochromocytoma. Sending this man home with the same medications that have already failed is not a plan — it is negligence.

options.text: Admit to ICU for immediate surgery prep
options.feedback_wrong: ICU is not immediately necessary unless he is in active hypertensive emergency. He needs medical stabilization over 10-14 days before surgery. Admit to a monitored bed on the endocrine or medical service, not the ICU.

report_template.presenting: episodic attacks of severe headache, diaphoresis, and palpitations over 8 months, with a collapse this morning

pertinent_positives.addl-hx: paroxysmal episodes triggered by exertion and bending, lasting 20-40 minutes with complete resolution between attacks, 8 lb weight loss, family history of adrenal tumor
pertinent_positives.pe: BP 188/112, HR 108, flushed and diaphoretic, abdominal tenderness in right upper quadrant, hypertensive retinopathy on fundoscopy
pertinent_positives.labs: hyperglycemia glucose 148 without diabetes, mildly elevated troponin from catecholamine injury, frequent PVCs on EKG
pertinent_positives.imaging: CT abdomen showing 4.8cm heterogeneous right adrenal mass with central necrosis consistent with adrenal neoplasm
pertinent_positives.special: plasma free metanephrines 2.8 nmol/L and 24-hr urine metanephrines markedly elevated, confirming biochemical diagnosis of pheochromocytoma

report_template.plan: admit for alpha-blockade with phenoxybenzamine, endocrinology and surgical oncology consult, 10-14 day preoperative preparation, genetic testing for RET/VHL/SDHB mutations, laparoscopic adrenalectomy after adequate preparation

explanation: Pheochromocytoma is a catecholamine-secreting tumor of the adrenal medulla (or extra-adrenal paraganglia). The classic triad is episodic headache, sweating, and palpitations. Key: paroxysmal hypertension triggered by exertion, bending, or stress. Diagnose with plasma free metanephrines (most sensitive). Image with CT/MRI. Treat with alpha-blockade first (phenoxybenzamine), then beta-blockade, then surgery. Never biopsy. Never beta-block first.
patient_quote_addl_hx: "I've been in three campaigns. I don't get dizzy. I don't collapse. What happened this morning was — I was simply fatigued from the night exercise. My aide is prone to exaggeration. I'm here as a courtesy."
